label: 130_hospitals_hospital_patients_patient
description: Hospital mergers have been widespread in the US healthcare market since 1998. These consolidations affect competition and potentially the quality of care for patients, as studied by economists. Variations in health service use across regions also reflect long-standing debates over public versus private provision.

earliest_articles.title: Does Prescription Drug Adherence Reduce Hospitalizations and Costs?
earliest_articles.excerpt: Does Prescription Drug Adherence Reduce Hospitalizations and Costs? We estimate the impact of diabetic drug adherence on hospitalizations, ER visits, and hospital costs, using insurance claims from MarketScan® employer data. However, it is often difficult to measure the impact of drug adherence on hospitalizations…

earliest_articles.title: Public vs. Private Provision of Charity Care? Evidence from the Expiration of Hill-Burton Requirements in Florida
earliest_articles.excerpt: Public vs. Private Provision of Charity Care? Evidence from the Expiration of Hill-Burton Requirements in Florida This paper explores the consequences of the expiration of charity care requirements imposed on private hospitals by the Hill-Burton Act. newest_articles.title: Medicare Home Health Fraud: How Much, Where, and Who?
newest_articles.excerpt: Medicare Home Health Fraud: How Much, Where, and Who? How much fraud is there in Medicare and who commits it? We provide an answer for Medicare home health, a setting widely considered especially rife with fraud. We define a home health agency (HHA) as fraudulent if it was prosecuted by a federal strike force.…

newest_articles.excerpt: Understanding Medicaid Managed Care: The Procured Competition Model Medicaid is one of the largest public programs in the United States — providing health insurance to over 75 million low-income Americans — and over three quarters of its enrollees receive care via private “managed care” insurers. central_articles.title: Opening the black box of hospital mergers
central_articles.excerpt: Hospital mergers are a ubiquitous and ongoing phenomenon across US healthcare markets. Nearly 1,600 hospital mergers took place from 1998 to 2017 (Gaynor 2020). Mergers are a key topic of study because they influence competition, which has an outsize role in the quality of care received by patients (Gaynor and Propper…

central_articles.excerpt: For nearly a century, researchers have observed substantial geographic variation in the use of health care services. central_articles.title: A bitter(sweet) pill: The impacts of private provision of Medicaid
central_articles.excerpt: Whether public services should be provided directly by the government or contracted out to private firms is a central debate in public finance and public policy around the world.
